speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This second interview with Eugene Settle takes place at his home in Moscow on July 7th, 1975.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I was about twenty -two. I was twenty -six when I went in. I got age.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you happen to enlist?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I was drafted.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you were drafted?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yes, I was drafted. I went to Spokane to enlist in what the other kid enlisted. I had a friend that was in the Navy, he enlisted as a fireman. After he went in, he kept writing to me to come on and enlist in the Navy as a fireman. We might be lucky enough to get on the same boat. So I, in October 17th, I went up to enlist.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: They took me alright, but they couldn't take me in what I wanted to get in because I wasn't heavy enough. I think I had to wait a hundred and, I think a hundred and thirty -five,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: stripped to enlist as a fireman, and I only weighed a hundred and twenty -eight. But they told me that I could go in and get my, what did I have to say, they said there was. I could go in and I gained weight, well then I could, I could task for it, but I didn't do it either. Well, I came back home and waited for the draft about six months later.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And I was in Camp Lewis about, I don't know how long I was going to get my shots. I wasn't there, I didn't make a real, I wasn't there in about, just about a month.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Six weeks maybe, and it has plans for Camp Funston.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Camp what?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Camp Funston, Kansas. See how big up, there's another outfit that's ready to go overseas. And so we went over there, went from Camp Funston to Camp Merritt, and there about three days, and we went overseas. And then went overseas and we went up to Verdun, and the battle of Verdun was over with, but they're still moldering around there. And that's when they split us all up, some of the boys, we don't know what kind of replacement outfit. Some of the guys, they put in the ammunition train and some went in and task for it, given the different answers to the service. And you take this 100 and, I was, it was 100 and, about 160 of us guys that went for Camp Lewis, you know, we hadn't had any, we just, we hadn't had any training, you might say at all. So that is all, we drilled all right, but we hadn't been on, so a lot of guys hadn't been on the rifle range. Some of those kids out of the city, you know, they never saw a gun, you might say, until they got out there.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But, they said, Verdun, they divide us up, they picked some of the guys for the, sent them into the entity, and some of them, they sent the, the contamination train and one thing or another. And, of course, I was one of the guys, except me and myself and about 15 or 20 other fellas. We went out to, we'll count to do some advanced training.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And, so we went out there and took our gas mask training and rifle range and pistol range training and, and then, going about in October, that part of October, why, then they called us up, they called us up, looked at the doiner outfits, looked at the outfits, getting just a lot of smoke down up there, toward the end of the water rain and mud, so they'd reinforce us. So he's calling up, putting everybody behind the lines in, so that's when I went up. And I didn't have to go then, while Kenneth told me, he said, you don't have to go, if you don't. But, I wanted to go, so I went.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And I got up, we got up there, and now I go on hood, and we was up there, we were supposed to, we got up there on the, on the evening of the tent.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: This is right, that's the next day, the next day we went in there, and this is, this is ammunition, this is ammunition, and we expected our dog tags and everything, and we used to go, we were, we were, we were supposed to go in on the, on the night of the 11th. In the morning of the 11th hour, which is a sign, so you'll see how close I missed it.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: You'd get it going in, while that night, while we turned around, they turned us around and started, well, it was on the night of the 10th. The 10th, the evening of the 10th, evening of the 11th, that's when we were evening of the 11th, they turned us around. We used to go in that night, and then see the, going ahead while they just, what, just about faced and started showing back again. They knew his arms were signed the next morning, so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you feel about missing the combat? Were you relieved or were you sorry?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I, I kind of wanted to get under fire. I didn't get to close, I got under artillery fire, but I never got under, I kind of like to get under fire. All right, all right, I kind of, I kind of wanted, I was bad, I got, I was bad, I got up, I got up on them, got as close as I did, but I wasn't a mine guy, I kind of wanted. Well, I'll tell you what, when I went in the Army, you know, I wanted to, I wanted to get in combat, and I wanted to get wounded so I could have a Purple Heart. That was why I decided, because I never did think I was going to get killed. I didn't feel that way, but I never really thought they were going to kill me. But I wanted, I wanted to get wounded, and that's why I could get a Purple Heart.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, your chance is pretty good at that if you got in combat. Oh, yeah, yeah, I think so.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Pretty good. I'm getting some pretty good push. Now, when I, when we was up there, and we went up there long, long, just before the Army was assigned, it was a running battle, you know, the Germans were on retreat, and we was after them, and then all along was there. They would, they would put a machine gun nest over here, and one over here, just a skeleton army behind them, and see, the cops told us back, see. While the main army was getting away, see, that's the way they did it. They had one, probably four, five or six men there fighting the whole company. The machine guns, you see. And of course, now, they tell me that, as I found out after Army was assigned, they tell me that, that we was, we was losing. About six men to one at that rate. Because they was covering so much of, so many of us with, with such, so few events, see, with machine guns.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So I don't know why I might have, I mean, we got a Purple Heart and not ever been able to wear it. Because, of course, a lot of, lots of them got rifle wounds all right, too, so that's a concern. But the biggest majority of, the biggest majority of the wounded was from trap notices. It's all my fault.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like in the camps there, the Army camps in Europe?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I'll tell you, I didn't, and I didn't know much. We got in, we got into restaurants, and it was raining, and they put us out there just building the camp there at rest, and I got in there in September and 8th, and it was just some mud hole, and they just got a bar of cringe. The night before, we got in there one evening, we stepped in the mud that night, and then the next day, the next night, they had a bar of cringe enough so we could get in out of mud, and we just got in to it on, and got our whip rankings spread out on the floor, and then the officer stepped in there and said, be ready to roll in half an hour. So I didn't get to spend no night in Old Tampa. But after the armed forces, I came back as far as breast, breast, not breast, breast, but Long yaw in France, we fought an armed occupation that was going up on Orion, we fought them back, fought them back up to Long yaw in France, that's just out of Belgium. Got a camp there, and that's why I spent the winter of 1919. I thought I was going to get to come home, but I didn't. So, there was one company of 139th Infantry there. There's a veterinary company there, they had a lot of wounded, and gas horses there, they had a doctor. And then there was a company, there was three companies there, and there was two companies of American soldiers, one infantry outfit, one veterinary outfit, and one French company there. French company didn't stay there all winter, they just stayed there after Christmas, and maybe, I imagine, they went home.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, that's about the only camp I was in, I spent the winter there, it was pretty good, we had old big barracks, and it wasn't too bad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the time when you were on advanced training?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh, when I was on advanced training over there that time, we stayed in a little French village, and we just, well, we slept in sheds and barns there, and I might have put us up over here in the attic of a French home before I stayed.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: We did guard duty every night there, we had the road went right through this little village, and we had a guard there, there was an interest in the village all the time, and then off the day he had to go, he just had to walk from one end to the other, and see what's going on, you know, and just took a distance about here. And we had to visit each man, each guard, every hour, and so that's all we did, and then we practiced putting on a gas mask, we had to have a chamber of the tunnel, it was full of gas, and we'd have to put on a gas mask and go through it, and say not only that, and I hope I can, you know, just put a soft life. For in the middle of the war. Yeah, for in the middle of the war.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the company integrated?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, no, not, no.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, no, but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the officers black too?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, we, they were most officers, most officers, most officers were black, not the only, wait, but not the only black, black, all I saw in Orlando and France, now they were medical, or the staff, or the dental, something like that, doctors, stuff like that. But for the officers, I don't believe it.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I don't believe it. I know there wasn't any in NARA, but I think there was in, I think there was in, what they call the, quartermaster corps. I believe in the quartermaster corps, they was nothing to say that black officers, they didn't do nothing, they unloaded ships, stuff like that, you know. I think, I think there was some black officers in the quartermaster corps.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did they, did most of the guys come from, were they westerners?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, no, unless the guys got out there and most of them were from Texas. Well, I went from here to Camp Lewis, and of course the boys, the guys that was in Camp Lewis, were from Montana, Idaho, and California. Most of them were from California, you might know. And then we left Camp, we left Camp Fort Lewis and went to Camp Ponce and Candace, while there were these guys, that's one of the Texas guys, and Texas two from Camp Ponce, and none of them, from Camp Travis, Texas, which was up there. And after we got the field in, we started out on rifle range there one morning, and we hadn't got the rifle on, and we called back and we went to sale, and then we went to, we went from there to Camp Merritt, New Jersey.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know when you were going in there, that that's the way that it was, that it wasn't going to be any greater?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, I knew that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I knew that, and this war went in. When I was drafted here, it came on the paper that I would be sent to, I wouldn't be able to, I wouldn't go with them, with the Moscow boys, because I would be, they had to be sent to some camp in the south, or whatever. So, some of them tried to do something about it, but it didn't resolve.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: The door worked out pretty well for me anyway, I had to get in and tie in a pretty good outfit, 139th Infantry, put a little time on it, and then when that was over, I got a bunch of guys come in from this, from the draft, last draft I guess, and they brought them over there, and they were dismantling airplanes and stuff, and stuff like that, and tearing down buildings, and they had to sit home, and so I got, I was transferred over there, in the spring, and the main division that I was with, they came home in the first two years, you see, and some of these guys, like myself, was, got in there late, like, and I was a sergeant, and that's one thing, they needed some commission to come off with this new watch that came in, so I got pulled into that, and then we went off to L 'Ou for a while, and I was a pretty good athlete in school, and they started to get up an athlete, get to have a track team, and so I got on the track team, and from March, until 1st of July, I didn't do nothing but just travel, all France, wherever these American troops was, this track team went on a decoration day, in 1919, I ran in a wheelie race, from Chiaotius to Paris. And the race ended at the Archer triumph in the parish. And I ran the last three rounds. It was on decoration day, 1919. And so they called us back about the 1st of July that they were gonna release the prisoners. I went back to company and on the 5th of July, I went to broke camp and started from home.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And I got, I got homelanded in the United States, ran back from the American's Hall on August, August the 5th, 1919.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, it was quite a year to remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of impressions did you have of the French? Well, well, what did they think of us?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I think they thought we thought they was pretty, well, some of the soldiers, they didn't think too much of. Well, they thought they had plenty of money and they were kind of, I think they thought they were, thought they were a little better than anybody else. And that's what they did. They pretty good though, the French, but I like the French people, it's forever.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you saying that our boys did or the French, the French thought they were better?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, our boys did. It's in no tears, but of course they, Cushing took the American army as the best, best paid army in the world. I never, never could get anything in this. And so they, they get, they get pretty rowdy sometimes. I think that a lot better.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did your family feel about having people living in their attic?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh, they didn't object to that. He's very nice, very nice. Where I was anyway was I, he didn't have to go through the house. I just put a little ladder up on the outside and went up the ladder. I just had it. I saw, you take nights when I was on guard, baby. I always, I noticed it, when I see it a little light in the back of the house all the time, they leave, they don't have no, they didn't allow much light there, you know, it was back in line by the way, but they had to be careful with the lights, you know. But I could see how, I'd have to walk right past this place where I stand. I just feel like I could, see just a little dim little light back there. That was what it was all about, and that night, early morning, when I really like, I got off guard, do that, come on, start up the ladder. This old French lady, she come out and took me by the arm and took me in the back end of the room there, like she had, she had some welcome bread for me. Like, for like, off the ship. Very nice old lady. That's good, man. That old French bread was pretty hard to beat through in mine open.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the guys that you were with in that, in your unit, did you, did you?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I'll tell you.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I didn't, I had more trouble with them than anybody. I just couldn't, hard for me to adjust myself, because everybody was doing things, and I was, I don't, I didn't take a drink, but I'm not a drinker, and never was. And I never was a rowdy, and I, I always talked through respect, and I, so this all the lighted, I cut out a while I, and I decided to adjust myself there,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: because we be in the black, but I never, I never was around in any, because I left, I left my parents to walk here when I was, I was five years old, around five years old, so. And I, I never, and so I, I never was around any black people myself, outside my family, until after I was, well, I guess I was in high school, I went to Spokane at the time, and met some people there, but, so it was quite a, it was quite a shock to me, to kind of get out, and then I did another thing, that I found out, after, there was always a division point, after we went over, after these were, it's 150th of us from Camp Louis, so it took us to Camp Louis and Kansas, and met these guys from Camp Travis, some of them had been in the Army over a year, but they were all black, what did they, there's always kind of a division point, between the western boys and the soldier up in the south, I don't know how it was, and I found that out, and I had my brother, the youngest brother, he, he finished college here in 29, well, he left high school, soon he got enough credits to get in college, for he didn't really graduate, he just didn't got enough credits, he quit college and took a short course, very course, and then he, then he went and, he went out and worked in Creamery's, around over the country, worked up a sand point here for quite a while, and then after he worked out, worked at that two or three years, then he decided to come back and finish college, and he come back and finish college, and he finished in 29,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and then, he went, he got his job at, Virginia State College of Petersburg, Virginia,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and they let him, they let him, they took on him as assistant professor there, but he just, he just, he just had a BS, and so he came back the next year, and went to WSU and got his, got his master's, and he got a little better job, and when he went after that, then after he left to, started to make something, he went to Cornell, started to work on his PhD, and he never did finish his PhD, he finished everything but his thesis, he never did get his thesis, and so on, so he, you know, he called him Dr. Settle all the time, but he never did get his, he never did finish his, he never did get his thesis, so, that happens to a lot of people, making everything done with that. Yeah, he passed away before he was out of his business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He stayed there at Cornell?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, he just went up there in the summertime, and went and worked on his PhD, he was teaching at Virginia State College, at Petersburg, Virginia, but he went to school in Cornell in the summer months, that's why he was working on his PhD at Cornell.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you were starting, you were starting to talk about him, you were talking about the difference between Southerners and Western.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh yeah, that's what I, then he went, then he bought a big land sale, Petersburg was laying 667 acres that we was in that place, and he's going to stock one, he's an animal, an hose is what he was teaching to me, and he had some purebred stock, and once you know that, it was hard for him to get,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: help out on his farm there, because he was a Yankee, and yeah, he didn't like it, yeah, so that's the reason, well I was down there, and I guess in 1962 I was down there, that's what my fifth down there, I went down there, so I had a stroke and a sprain, so that boy went down, and threw down the risk of him, and I was out there on his farm, and in a couple old Virginia mountain, mountaineers, he raised a short horned cattle, on his farm out there, and they come down there, they wanted to buy a heifer, out of his strain, and they came down there, and he was, man, I was there, and he hadn't got out there yet, and I was out there, of course I, he's having some work to get on his house, and he had me out there, I was kind of supervising him for him, tell you what I was saying, these old bastards, these old bastards, they came down all the hills up there, and they wanted some, on his heifer, he was looking for a duck to settle, and I told him, he wasn't here yet, I said, I'm his brother, and they were like, and then he says, you didn't think I would know, they didn't think about it, but he had a heifer there, that they were interested in, they wanted some of that same bloodline, he had, and so he talked a lot of while, and then I'd say my brother came, and they went out to the, they went out to Barnyard, they had a look at the cows, and of course my brother had been down there, see that is in 62, and he's been down there since 29, and all these old guys asked him,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: who I was, he told him to ask him, he said,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: well he said,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: so if you want to do any business with them, they better get rid of him,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: so if you know, you go down there, and the tune's open, and your mouth, but they know you don't belong there, that's the way they knew, and they didn't know I didn't belong there, that's the way they did anything, oh yeah, yeah. It's kind of surprising, but it's true, and he tried to get different families, to move out there on his face, and work for him, he didn't want to do it. Because they knew he was a Yankee. Yeah, mhm.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: What goes, when I was down there, though he's in there, I don't know, because he was southern of them, now he's in, let's see, and you stay down there, it's still very long, you get that, quiet, I draw all of you too, you know, a lot of you do it a little bit, I didn't see him much longer, but he had to go. But anyway, they didn't know he was, they had adopted him by that time.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: when you were over there in Europe, did you find that you had a harder time with the guys in your troop, because you were a westerner, not a southerner?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, as I say, there's kind of a little division of art, I had a little rough time, in a way, I had a rough time in this way, I had two strikes against me, and I was, that is in the company, out of it, I was a westerner,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and I was about one of the smallest guys in the outfit, and I was a sergeant.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Two of them was, you know, it kind of made a difference, it kind of,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: didn't like me giving them orders too well, but that didn't bother me, I didn't let that work, and I gave them the same, and I got them done, because they so often didn't like it, but there wasn't much they could do about it.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I didn't, I wasn't overbearing to anybody, I just did my job, and that was it, and whether, whether you're some south, or whether you're some west, it didn't make no difference to me, I had to treat them well, I didn't show no partiality nobody, I just did my job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Or you can say. And for a move, they saw pretty well, pretty well alike. I thought he was this, or that one, or even that one. That's what I told me, I didn't have to go to this month, the guys are with me, they just put up a sit -and -all about me not going, and of course I wanted to go anyway, and of course that's the only way to go, so anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why were they saying that you didn't have to go?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: They thought I was too young, thought I was too young, they just think I was about 16.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think that, let's say that the westerners were more intended than the southerners, or wasn't there any difference like that?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I don't know, I don't know if there's much difference in that only thing I would say that,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: it seemed to me like it was just the other way around, seemed like it, I don't know whether it's a general attitude or the southerners, but it's just because those guys from Texas, you know, the Texas, well, she figured he's from Texas, but he's nothing that compares to Texas, no way she performed, of course if you're from Texas, why don't you hear a top cat?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, that's, oh yes, there's Idaho, well, there's no Idaho that's on the map, and you see them, they're not watching, why they're watching DC.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's a funny deal, they know about Montana, but Idaho and Washington, they know nothing about Idaho, it's just complete loss, but Washington, when you say anything about Washington, they'd say, well, they think you talk about Washington DC, but they all knew about Montana, everybody I'd ever seen, I don't have them, what do you say?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: When they say about Montana, they knew Montana, but they didn't know Washington and Idaho, Washington was Washington DC and Idaho just didn't exist.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: My head doesn't surprise me, it's still pretty bad, in the east they don't know. Yes it is, of course I was in the east. When I was down there, I guess it was last time, when I guess that's in 62 I was down there, I wanted to buy a pair of shoes, it was whether I was up in Richmond, Virginia, we went to a big partner store there, and I'd get the pair of shoes, and of course as I say, if I go down there, to the open of your mouth, they knew you don't belong there, and of course that's what the next thing, you want to know where you're from.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So I was buying a pair of shoes there, and so did I, the painting floor, and he said, so I said, are you, if he asked me if I was an educator, and I thought no, obviously I was going to be around going to college down there, I don't know why I was here. He was supposed to ask me, you want to get down here in Richmond, or yeah, and I thought no yes, that's my brother, my brother's college over there, and I was down in Richmond, and he wanted to know where I was from, and I told him I was from Idaho, oh he says, my son -in -law,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: isn't from Idaho, but he said he's right close to it, and I said, where is he from, Montana?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He said, I said no, he said no, he said Washington, he said no, Oregon, he said no, no, and he, he started a little sized, yeah, he's from Nebraska.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh, yeah, a lot of he's coming out always to go up to yet Idaho.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, there's no according to me, That's across the Mississippi River down there. You lose a lot of those photos. You're in a wide open space.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, even Texas is the west as much as it's the south. Yes, it is. Texas is the southwest.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yes, it was quite an experience for me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you feel that you grew up a lot while you were over there?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I did. I grew up a lot when I was over there. And the kid and I out here, he was from Baton Rouge, Louisiana. And he was a mixture of Negro and French, and Creole, they called them. He could speak, he spoke French, but he didn't speak a... So French could understand you, but it wasn't quite the way they speak it, than the native tongue. In Creole, they had a thought of a... They spoke in a little different tongue than they speak in France, and so on. He and I was walking... He was on the track team, too. And he was walking down the street from the end, so we met at the start of the French cafe, the Sidal Cafe. And this friend, he was a black girl sitting at the table there. And of course, we thought, this kid, he could speak French. And she asked me, he just left me up to come here. We could sit there and sit down there. And of course, he got talking to this girl in French, you know.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And pretty soon, we come to find out that she could speak better English than he could.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: She's going to be an educator in London.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: She's prepared for an English, and so he talked there quite a while, and she told us... She told us that she felt that she was superior to us to the American people. And she's not even out here, but she's African. She's African anyway. Anyway, she told us that... Yeah, they felt that they were superior to the American people. It was simply that the American Negro parents had been slaves.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I wanted to tell her, but I didn't. Don't read your one. They didn't get yours.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, she told me she was pretty hot stuff. Well, I don't... I don't realize anyone has got any pride. Because I think it's a pride, there's a spice of life. You don't have any pride, there's not much to you.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I had lots of... that's a year to remember, for me that year and on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's just too bad that you still had to feel superior to somebody else. Yeah, yeah. It sounds like the same old story. Yeah.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But there's very few people that I don't care... Oh, black and white, that spent a year in France and got away with it as easy as I got it. I wouldn't have got what I wanted to get. words: And see, this just had a gal going on there, who was, who was, who was, who was, who was together, and he jumped on out of there and left his gun on there.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And this man, oh, he said, you know, he said, gimme a, gimme a lift, you know, you know, he said, well we had a lot of trouble, I said, yeah, I'll tell you something else. He was kind of holding up the way down, he said, yes, we are. So,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: this other kid was with me anyway. We kind of, I got a hold of him and he got a hold of her up on there and somewhere around there and you know, and after he sat down there and they said that he was going to sleep and we were just sitting around there and kept sitting around there and they saw the kids around there and then this kid was rolling down on his, that's about him, he was almost his soul there and I got a hold of that and he said,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I don't know who was who or what but anything, he said, I should give someone's phone anyways, a girl around here, if he wants to, and I don't know, I said, down here, so he could learn a little better, he said, I think we should make it, but I don't know where to go, I can't stay too good and she can't stay at all, he said, we didn't have a heck of time, well, I said, if it's all right with her, why, she says, I picked her out, so I did, I took her out before a time or two and I turned over and saw the kid, he took her out during a time or two and,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and so that was all right. I don't know whether it was the next Saturday night or whether it was night that I was there, but this old kid, he was hitting him, he's the same guy, he walked in the hallway and he was drunk, he had a drink or two of them, I don't think it was the next Saturday night, as far as he was concerned with that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He was standing there at the hallway and I walked in, I don't know who was with me that night or whether I was alone, anyway, he used to talk to some other kids there and he said,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: anyway, he said, I walked past and he said, well, God damn you, he said, you tried to take my girl away from me the other night, I just turned around and I just walked up to him and I just got him right like that and I shoved him up against the wall and I just, about, about 15 minutes, and I said, say it again and I said, I'll lock every goddamn tooth you got right down here, so, you're a lying bastard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't God you ever see a man so rough now?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He, I've all apologized and he did to me, he sure did. I told him I made him tell him like it was, he did too.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I understood everybody and I didn't pick your color but I did feel an idea. I don't let nobody, don't cut around and push me around. He just swore.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So that's one reason I get along good with everybody. I'm out of a little bearing, I think everybody's right, but I demand my attention.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm a little bit, you know, myself, I was more Jewish. Yeah, and when I went to school, I was the only Jewish kid in the school and I went to.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And every once in a while, you know, not often, but every once in a while, some kid would throw that out to me.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He just didn't like me and was using that as a way to get out of there. Yeah, sure. Yeah, I know what that is, guys. I heard my daughter taught school in Oakland down there for 10 years and I heard her pick the same way, I can see her down.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: She said the black kids, they just told up to them too and she said, and the Jewish kids, they run into the same problem all that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, do you know this Barbara Freeman that works down here at the Crossroads work store?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: She's a little Jewish girl from Detroit.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: She is born, she's a Jewish, Jewish, Jewish, and she's turned Christian.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: She's got a head for that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, I was wanting to ask you about the hanging that you did. About the hay baler that took her around the country. I guess I was George Johnson was the guy that told me, wanted me to say hello to you for him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Remember George Johnson?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: George Johnson, I know so many Johnsons. He's the one from the Juliaetta now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I guess he used to live right near Linville or not too far from there because he said that, I don't know if he worked with you, with your crew or your bail on his place or something.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: My dad was a real old country when I was a young man. I left the hay baler and went to the Army. He was a real old country. He was a real old country. He lived here for a year and a year and a year all over the country. But George Johnson,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: did he used to live around Land...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Genesee? Or do you know him?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I know he was... he was living near Linville when you were out in that neck of the woods. But I don't know him very good. I just met him at a friend's house. He was a friend of mine. He was visiting. Yeah, I think he was a half a day in the George, half a day in the Ohio and all. At least I'm pretty sure that George is. He's the only George... one of the only Johnsons right around Juliaetta. But anyway, I was going to ask you about the hay baling and what kind of an operation you had there and how that worked.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, it looked just the same as... we went from farmer to farmer and baled hay, whatever farmer had in hay. He contacted my dad or had contact to him. So we just went... just like we used to do in the country. He went farm to farm and he was, as it was, all over the country. And then he baled hay for Pullman College and he baled a straw, rather. And for a year he always built a bunch of straw to build for the... either the Idaho College or over the WSU, so this animal has a department. They use lots of straws. He baled lots of straw for both colleges.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And he custom -baled all over the country, sure, from... from Palouse up here. I don't think we ever went beyond Palouse very much. He went down to Tennessee and grew down to the... grew down to the breaks before he started down Lewiston Hill there. We just baled all over the country.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And my father and his three of us boys, and he hired about two or three fellas.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Let's see, what do we have?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: We have this here and this here.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, there's about six of us on the crew.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And he had a farm out here in the northeast of town where his kids grew up. He raised lots of berries of different kinds and garden stuff. So he wore the strawberries and did that. And then he had this hay baler. This hay baler was on the side, the sideline.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the season for Bayland now?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, we started... it started on about the 1st of July and then we bailed up until snow comes because we baled straw. He always had a contract with either the Idaho College or WSU and had a straw. And in those days, they stationed and tried to make both the straw and big stacks, you see. And then we come on and bailed. And the college was buying these stacks, buying these straws, and then we went out and bailed out until snow flies come through. We went up in November. We started out on the 1st of July, up here.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And then, of course, we got that, did that for the year.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Because some people thought he was doing good. Yeah, I thought he was making too much money. So let's try it. But it's hard work. Nowadays, there's no hard work to bailing. They just go out in the field and win the award and pick it up and bale it right out in the bale. And there's no hard work here. They're going along and falling along picking up the bales now. Those days, we bailed it out and we set it to the bale and a hay field. And then the farmer that we bailed for, he hauled a hay to the bale. And he just had about half a dozen, one by 12 planks nailed together. We call it a slip, you might say. And then they'd load it on those planks and have about four or six of those planks. They'd pull up alongside the baler and then they'd put a rope right in front of the bale, right in front of the load. Fast one to the wheel of the bale and another to the post out here. And then drive up and the board just, the board just flipped right off one of the hay, you know. And that's the load of hay right there on the ground by the bale. And then it's up to our crew to get up and pitch it off into the bale and run from the hay. We're just going to put it straight through it. So it took about, see there's about three of us, about six of us on the bale. And then when the farmer, he'd have to have about, he had to have about three men to, about two of those slips unless he already had already stacked. He had to have about three men to load into one or two slip, so he'd get off the field, fishing before he'd survive the slip. So it was quite an operation. And then the sun got hotter around the whole hay, we'd order them anywhere else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, now, would you just do, would you be able to do very much in a day as far as the farm?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, we bale.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So we used to bale around about 15, 20 tons a day.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But you see,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I think that most of the time it was around about, it was around about three and a half a ton, but we got to be able to. So there was no big money, any of you had to buy your wire.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you sleep out doing that, just like if you was thrashing through?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, once in a while we did, but we used to sometimes, most of the time we came home at night, we'd come home at night. We first started though, we stayed out, always, stepping to Farmer's Barn or something like that, you know. Always bale and stroll, but usually bale and stroll, that's pretty late in the fall of the year. We usually came home from bale and stroll, but we switched to the bale hay lots of times, where we would sleep right out in the field, right out to the bale over there. And the last time we'd get up early morning and probably bale, probably an hour before we always went in for breakfast. Two in the morning.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And when did you start doing this before you went into the war?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, yeah, yes. My father just started doing that before I went into the war. Well, I tell you, when father started to bale in the, I believe it was 1912, he bought an old bale out of sale. In fact, the matter, he just wanted to do his own bale in and anything else. He was an old thing, a bow and a chain, and he got up a little and nothing. He bought it for sale and started to bale and then buying a bale for himself and by golly his neighbor crossed the fifth hill out here, and they all felt the name Nelson.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, John, why don't you go and bale mine?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So dad did. Well, before he got through that, somebody wanted him to bale his, you know, some other neighbor and farmer right there wanted to bale his, and so by golly and all that, he did quite a little bale in that with that old bale out of here. And it started out the next year, but I wanted to bale John, someone bale my hay, bale my hay. So he wanted that in the first year and the second year, and then the guy said, oh, I should get him and get the bale up on him and pile it up on him, so he just went and bought him a bale there, bought him a new one, a new one, a new outfit, and that started it. Then he run that one for I don't know how many years.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Then he got rid of the horse power and bought him a gas truck with a gas motor on it. And I don't know how many, I think he had about two of those.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you boys make pretty good money when you were as far as wages go, working out of the house?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, all I could do was speed it very much.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: You couldn't speed very much if we had lost them. And I say, I think it was about three or three and a half tons is what he baled, and we made about 20 tons in there, especially if it wasn't very much for him. But we made a low.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And then we used to have to hire about two men.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get to know the country pretty good when you were doing that?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, yes, they say so.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yes, they sure knew the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And we knew every place to hire your bales in the country where they'd bring you out a lunch about the fiddle before, don't you?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, they sure knew that. The place is where we vealed to where we got a lunch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just some places would be good enough to feed you while you were there?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh, yeah, they didn't feed us while we were there. Oh, yeah, some places where...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: They'd rather... I was a old couple, some weathers, they'd rather... They'd rather feed a little more for the bale, and then less for the shells and feed us while we get back to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember much of what those places were like as far as how many...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it people every 160 acres in them days?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, well, I'll tell you.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: The grocery store was only for a start, you know. The farms weren't as large as they are now. There was lots of 160 acres of farms and 250 because there was mostly farm horsepower. There weren't very many factories where they started out that way. No, no. So that's what put my dad out of business is when they got this tractor business, the combine business, you see. And then they got these power balers where you just cut the hay and then windrow it, then just go out out to the baler and pick it up and bale it right out in the field and just drop the bales along the stream.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: That's... Well, my dad, he never... Well, he was an old man then. He was the truest of balers by that time. But that's what came along. We just started about doing that a little bit before last year or two that he baled. He started building that. But he is about done anyway, so anyway, that's...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But how were these farms doing when you, in the teens, when you started baling? Were most of them in pretty good shape or were the farmers struggling to get by?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I'll tell you this.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: It was kind of a struggle. It's like it is now. Some of them were pretty well. Most of them were... Just most of them that seem like they... They never had too much money and the farmers didn't seem like that from about 19... Well, let's see, a little depression.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I've heard it said that after World War I there that there never did come back for farmers. The 20s were hard years for them. Yeah. That's true.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah. So I come back in the 20s there and I... I only baled in this one... I kept with the baler just a little while after I came back from the service. A couple years. Then I got buried and I went to farm and loved myself. And I never did help on the baler. And they only... Listen, they had a... Lots of times, you know, in the wintertime, some people would have a barn full of hay and they'd want to bale it to the barn. Because I'd take checks for help. I used to go out and help them just to get a job like that. Here too, I just got help from the cattle here. Barn.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: A lot of people didn't seem to have... After the war, I'd say they seemed like everything went up before the war. The machinery and stuff. And it never did come down. And then they had this substitute flower. So we never did come back.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Like when we first started, it goes everything... When we first started, it was a year or another way.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Everything was horses and there wasn't any practice. There wasn't any practice in the country then.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Everything was horse power. And there's lots of hay. We had bale alfalfa, we bale templated, we bale grain hay. We had all kinds of hay to bale and there's lots of it. So you might say that every farmer...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Most of them always had a little hay to bale. Some of them would fill the barn and if they had any left, they'd bale it and so forth. And then they had a pack of wheat that had too many wild oats in it. But they didn't want to cut that for hay to sell.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Of course, hay at that time is old about food. $15, $20. Good hay. Now it's fast food. It got up to $80 a ton.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Most of your jobs are small ones then?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, we had some pretty good jobs, big jobs. So that is what I mean. We had several... I know we had two hundred or a few jobs while there was alfalfa, probably 30 or 40 at the field. Something like that. Most of them was 5, 10 acres, like this, perhaps. In fact, they never... Another farmer, a very few farmers had too big a field of alfalfa. That's why we had quite a little bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Of course, a farmer had horses, eh?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: A lot of farmers didn't like to feed alfalfa to the horses. They figured it was too washy. They used to throw a good cow feed, but they didn't like to feed it to horses. And then before harvest, in crop, they'd usually mow, take the mower machine and cut about four sloths around that wheat field, you see. And then they'd go out.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: That's what they feed the horses. Yeah, that's what they feed the horses. Feed that to the horses. Feed the cattle alfalfa.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And then they'd go out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do many places still have the Homestead cabins on them?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, they got you. I don't know any... I don't believe this. I don't know a place in the country now that has a Homestead cabin on it. I don't believe I did it since you mentioned it.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But along about 1912, we started to feel hey, there was lots of places that had the old Homestead cabins were still there. But I don't know one of them. I don't believe anyone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were most of the families big? Or did they all different kinds?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I'll tell you, in those days, most of the families were pretty fair sized. I'll tell you, you know, this country is where I grew up, and then around Moscow here, it's kind of like the toys. It's a pretty Scandinavian country, you know. Now you take the hill here in Orksey, where I grew up, that was the fact of all the Scandinavian countries. And all of them had pretty good size families. And of course the hill over here towards Blaine country in there. And then a little further this way, a little further west down the top, Thorn Creek country. That was around Uniontown, Golden Johnson. That's the German settlement in there. And that's where you saw your good horses and your larger farms and good crops. That's around Uniontown and Golden, that was the German settlement in the end. They had some nice farms down there. And they had some nice stock.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was it that they got ahead?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is it because they were in prairie and not...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, yeah, that's what they got. That's in the tourist country down through there, you see. And of course you take it out this way toward that's the Wolf's Lake Ambre. And that's not given them off anything until here in the last... Oh, since World War II, you might say, they got the using fertilizers. I can remember you took it up that toy up there. I worked for the L 'Into Conograin course for 20 years. And they got a warehouse up that toy and they had a little warehouse there. And if they got 20, 30 Bush's Lake or what, why they got a big crop. And since World War II, they have increased that warehouse at Troy and Duryville about three times. And they use fertilizer up there and they get their 75 and 80 Bush's Lake and crop the same thing down here. And that fertilizer is made of country. They sure did something for the Troy country out this way. Because this country this way and this way and this way, that's all prairie country. This timber soil, you take the timber off of it, it seems like I don't want to say... I heard the old timers say that it leaves a certain amount of turban tine in the soil. And it takes no years before it gets up. You don't produce too good a crop. Since they got even fertilizer on it, the way they produce it gets good a crop as anywhere.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it easy on the prairie? Was it easy on the prairie country to start farming right away? It was hard to clear that brush, you know?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, no, no brush on it for prairies. Now you take my wife's dad down here, he came up from... He came up from South Carolina to Madeira, California. I thought he was down in the Fresno country, this side of it. She started working for a big cattle. I love her mixin'. I can't call her name this big cattle. And they had people handling their horses too. Miller & Lutz, we own thousands and thousands of each of the land here. So they leased them after me and we farmed them in the prairie.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And jealous about her.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And her brother -in -law was like, they couldn't get along. They farmed together, so she wanted to get out of there. So tell his dad she got the money he could get a little stuff out of his brother. He's selling out to him. He came up in the wall a while or something.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And you know, in the early days, the railroad company, the government gave the railroads every other quarter section of land when they built the railroad through here. Well, he came up from the wall -a -wall country and of course he didn't have no money, but he had a promise of getting some more from his brother back there. And he bought 106 acres from a railroad company down here, Dodge of Washington. You know where Dodge is, down below Lewis and Trine Dodge and Thummeloy and other side of it. Well, that's where my wife grew up. He bought a bunch of grass. There was no grass on it, it was a bunch of grass. You heard a bunch of grass. That's the ending of the buffalo thing they were on in those days. And he bought this 160 -year -old bunch of grass here. And he talked to Bank and an Ocean, and he wanted some money to get a cloud. He didn't have a team or nothing. And they plowed. He got some plows in there early in the fall. And they plowed there all winter. He plowed that 160 up. And then in January, I guess in February, in February he ceded the wheat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The whole thing?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: The whole thing, 160 acres. And that next year, that fall, he sold and got enough wheat off that. He paid for his 160s. And he had borrowed money to buy, got any money in. And he bought all his debts, regardless of whether he put plow on it and seed and everything. And he paid everybody. And right out of here, he bought him an off -fit and another 160. And sent for his family and built the house. And he did pretty good the next year. And then he kept on building, kept on buying, and he had 1 ,300 acres of land in his name down there at one time. And then he actually got the family there. And his boys grew up from the U .S. Creek farmers down there. And then he got a couple of dry years in a row down there. Just about broke the old fella.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And his boys got sick on him. And he had sold off, got rid of the, I think he lost part of it. It was so very good. And he left that in and left your boughs there. He lost both of his boys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He died?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, he lost both his sons. And then they were getting old and they started to farm him horses. He had to, my wife and her sister, they were the only two kids that was home. And they wanted him to, when he got out of all the walls out there, they wanted him to just let the kids shift themselves and then buy them a small ranch that they could take care of. They two of them, you know, get them cows and chickens. He wanted to farm. He was a big farmer. He thought he'd do it again. So he lost his boys.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And so about the second year, Stell and I were married, but then he had to quit, so we took him over. We took him in with us until he died.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they stay for very long? Did they live for very long?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, they lived. She was young, mother of a couple. He passed away at 85 and I think he was, her dad was in his 80s when he died. I think that's what he was, 83 or 4.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did he get that prairie land?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, they came out here, they must come out here right after he did long, about 19, two or three, I think we were a little ahead of him. We got here about 98, about 90, come to Idaho. Of course, you know, when he came to Idaho, my dad could have come home to the land up here in Nez Perce County, up here on Nez Perce. He'd start farming and then we got to look into it, what is all, it was done with mountains. He was all right for cattle, I guess. He never cried. But if he'd have known it, when we first came here, when he was going home and he'd get up there in the big banks, if he'd known it, when he first stopped here then he might have looked into it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, that there was still prairie country?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: There was homestead land up there. I know a part of the guys, I know a part of the, got a homestead up there just about the time we came here, a little after we came here, it was good land, good land.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was around Nez Perce?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yes, yes, up around Nez Perce.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Boy, I'm hardly noticing that it's a hot summer day here.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, it seems so hot. Up in, quite a while after we got here, up in this Bovill up in that country there, around Pierce and up back in the land, we'd get these stone and timber cleaners. Up in there, there's a wheel at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Weren't some of those where the lumber company was paying people to... I don't know, I don't know about that. I've heard that from several people.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I know that we'd been here several years, and my dad and another guy moved a little woman up in that Bovill and she took up a stone and timber claim up there, on that outer Bovill somewhere there. She's going up there to spend the summer on it to get food up for her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I know he moved up there and on whom brought her back?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But I guess...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she at all concerned about going up there alone to spend the... Well, I guess that she was a widow, and she wasn't married to this guy, but I guess they were fixed to get married.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He went away from up there. He had his team and rig. He didn't stay up there all the time, but... I don't know what he was doing here in Washington, what his business was, but anyway, he moved up there with him. And Dad left. He left him up there. And I think... I forget what his name was. Dad knew him. He got acquainted with him on the way up there. He told my father that he kind of went back and forth up there in the summertime while she was up there. He kept his back on. They were just going to get married, but they weren't married then.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: She had two or three kids. He was nine years, a seven -year -old kid.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: My dad said he saw some flammable on that place up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Pretty good timber, but you couldn't farm it, probably.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, you couldn't farm with it. No, I couldn't farm with it. But I know a fellow that built this Duma seed out there. They were in the Wilson House. He came here and he started working at a grocery store here.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: For a man by the name of Washburn. And a little man in Washburn, he sent him a message. He went up there and filed on a stone in timber claim. He lived up there and proved up for it. Then he married the old woman in Washburn's daughter.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Finally he sold his stone in timber claim. Then him and his father -in -law built this Duma seed company down here. That used to be Washburn Wilson. His name was Wilson.
